question:
B-1. Let \( S \) be a set and let \( \circ \) be a binary operation on \( S \) satisfying the two laws
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x \circ x=x \text { for all } x \text { in } S \text {, and } \\
(x \circ y) \circ z=(y \circ z) \circ x \text { for all } x, y, z \text { in } S .
\end{array}
\]

Show that \( \circ \) is associative and commutative.
solution:
B-1 Using the given laws we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
x \circ y & =(x \circ y) \circ(x \circ y)=[(x \circ y) \circ x] \circ y=[(y \circ x) \circ x] \circ y \\
& =[(x \circ x) \circ y] \circ y=(x \circ y) \circ y=(y \circ y) \circ x=y \circ x .
\end{aligned}
\]

From this commutative law we obtain
\[
(x \circ y) \circ z=(y \circ z) \circ x=x \circ(y \circ z)
\]

descriptive_long.question:
B-1. Let \( collection \) be a set and let \( \circ \) be a binary operation on \( collection \) satisfying the two laws
\[
\begin{array}{l}
elementalpha \circ elementalpha = elementalpha \text { for all } elementalpha \text { in } collection \text {, and } \\
(elementalpha \circ elementbeta) \circ elementgamma = (elementbeta \circ elementgamma) \circ elementalpha \text { for all } elementalpha, elementbeta, elementgamma \text { in } collection .
\end{array}
\]

Show that \( \circ \) is associative and commutative.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-1 Using the given laws we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
elementalpha \circ elementbeta & =(elementalpha \circ elementbeta) \circ(elementalpha \circ elementbeta)=[(elementalpha \circ elementbeta) \circ elementalpha] \circ elementbeta=[(elementbeta \circ elementalpha) \circ elementalpha] \circ elementbeta \\
& =[(elementalpha \circ elementalpha) \circ elementbeta] \circ elementbeta=(elementalpha \circ elementbeta) \circ elementbeta=(elementbeta \circ elementbeta) \circ elementalpha=elementbeta \circ elementalpha .
\end{aligned}
\]

From this commutative law we obtain
\[
(elementalpha \circ elementbeta) \circ elementgamma=(elementbeta \circ elementgamma) \circ elementalpha=elementalpha \circ(elementbeta \circ elementgamma)
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-1. Let \( labyrinth \) be a set and let \( \circ \) be a binary operation on \( labyrinth \) satisfying the two laws
\[
\begin{array}{l}
daffodil \circ daffodil=daffodil \text { for all } daffodil \text { in } labyrinth \text {, and } \\
(daffodil \circ saxophone) \circ pendulum=(saxophone \circ pendulum) \circ daffodil \text { for all } daffodil, saxophone, pendulum \text { in } labyrinth .
\end{array}
\]

Show that \( \circ \) is associative and commutative.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-1 Using the given laws we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
daffodil \circ saxophone & =(daffodil \circ saxophone) \circ(daffodil \circ saxophone)=[(daffodil \circ saxophone) \circ daffodil] \circ saxophone=[(saxophone \circ daffodil) \circ daffodil] \circ saxophone \\
& =[(daffodil \circ daffodil) \circ saxophone] \circ saxophone=(daffodil \circ saxophone) \circ saxophone=(saxophone \circ saxophone) \circ daffodil=saxophone \circ daffodil .
\end{aligned}
\]

From this commutative law we obtain
\[
(daffodil \circ saxophone) \circ pendulum=(saxophone \circ pendulum) \circ daffodil=daffodil \circ(saxophone \circ pendulum)
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-1. Let \( emptiness \) be a set and let \( \circ \) be a binary operation on \( emptiness \) satisfying the two laws
\[
\begin{array}{l}
constantone \circ constantone=constantone \text { for all } constantone \text { in } emptiness \text {, and } \\
(constantone \circ constanttwo) \circ constantthree=(constanttwo \circ constantthree) \circ constantone \text { for all } constantone, constanttwo, constantthree \text { in } emptiness .
\end{array}
\]

Show that \( \circ \) is associative and commutative.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-1 Using the given laws we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
constantone \circ constanttwo & =(constantone \circ constanttwo) \circ(constantone \circ constanttwo)=[(constantone \circ constanttwo) \circ constantone] \circ constanttwo=[(constanttwo \circ constantone) \circ constantone] \circ constanttwo \\
& =[(constantone \circ constantone) \circ constanttwo] \circ constanttwo=(constantone \circ constanttwo) \circ constanttwo=(constanttwo \circ constanttwo) \circ constantone=constanttwo \circ constantone .
\end{aligned}
\]

From this commutative law we obtain
\[
(constantone \circ constanttwo) \circ constantthree=(constanttwo \circ constantthree) \circ constantone=constantone \circ(constanttwo \circ constantthree)
\]

garbled_string.question:
B-1. Let \( lkjrevsn \) be a set and let \( \circ \) be a binary operation on \( lkjrevsn \) satisfying the two laws
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp \circ qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp \text { for all } qzxwvtnp \text { in } lkjrevsn \text {, and } \\
(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla) \circ mofnlcier=(hjgrksla \circ mofnlcier) \circ qzxwvtnp \text { for all } qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mofnlcier \text { in } lkjrevsn .
\end{array}
\]

Show that \( \circ \) is associative and commutative.
garbled_string.solution:
B-1 Using the given laws we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla & =(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla) \circ(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla)=[(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla) \circ qzxwvtnp] \circ hjgrksla=[(hjgrksla \circ qzxwvtnp) \circ qzxwvtnp] \circ hjgrksla \\
& =[(qzxwvtnp \circ qzxwvtnp) \circ hjgrksla] \circ hjgrksla=(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla) \circ hjgrksla=(hjgrksla \circ hjgrksla) \circ qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla \circ qzxwvtnp .
\end{aligned}
\]

From this commutative law we obtain
\[
(qzxwvtnp \circ hjgrksla) \circ mofnlcier=(hjgrksla \circ mofnlcier) \circ qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp \circ(hjgrksla \circ mofnlcier)
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\Omega$ be a non-empty set equipped with a binary operation $\star : \Omega\times\Omega \to \Omega$ satisfying the following two identities.

1.  (Interchange) \[(u\star v)\star w\;=\;(v\star w)\star u\quad\text{for all }u,v,w\in\Omega.\]
2.  (Idempotence) \[t\star t\;=\;t\quad\text{for all }t\in\Omega.\]

Prove that $\star$ is both commutative and associative on $\Omega$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1. Commutativity.
Fix arbitrary u,v\in \Omega .  Idempotence lets us duplicate their product:
  u\star v = (u\star v)\star (u\star v).   (1)
Apply the interchange law to the right-hand side of (1), viewing (u\star v), u, v as the three slots:
  (u\star v)\star (u\star v) = ((u\star v)\star u)\star v.   (2)
Another interchange with u,v,u in the slots gives
  (u\star v)\star u = (v\star u)\star u.   (3)
Insert (3) into (2) and simplify, using interchange and idempotence as needed:
  u\star v
  = ((v\star u)\star u)\star v
  = ((u\star u)\star v)\star v
  = (u\star v)\star v
  = (v\star v)\star u
  = v\star u.
Hence u\star v=v\star u; \star  is commutative.

Step 2. Associativity.
For arbitrary u,v,w\in \Omega  the interchange identity, followed by commutativity, yields
  (u\star v)\star w = (v\star w)\star u = u\star (v\star w),
which is the associative law.

Thus \star  is both commutative and associative on \Omega .  \blacksquare

core_steps: Duplicate an arbitrary product x∘y using idempotence: x∘y = (x∘y)∘(x∘y).
Apply the interchange law repeatedly to that duplicate to reverse the two factors, obtaining x∘y = y∘x (commutativity).
Insert commutativity into the interchange law to rewrite (x∘y)∘z as x∘(y∘z), giving associativity.

slot1.description: Name given to the underlying set.

slot2.description: Symbol chosen for the binary operation.

slot3.description: Letters used for the three generic elements in the laws.

slot4.description: Order in which the two axioms are listed in the statement.
